prompt: In several of our states, it is observed that nearly half of all adult citizens of suitable age and constitution have, by deliberate choice, declined to produce offspring. They are not barren by nature, but by disposition. What does this portend for republican government, which depends upon a virtuous and growing citizenry?
response:
This phenomenon you describe strikes at the very foundation of republican society, though I confess it presents difficulties that the political science of my age did not fully anticipate. A republic depends not merely upon the consent of the governed, but upon the continuance of the governed themselves — and upon their multiplication sufficient to sustain the institutions they have erected.

The ancient republics offer us some instruction here, though perhaps not the instruction we might wish. Rome in her later period witnessed a similar decline in the procreative disposition of her citizens, particularly among the more prosperous classes. Augustus found it necessary to enact laws penalizing the unmarried and childless, while rewarding those who produced offspring for the state. Yet these measures, however well-intentioned, could not restore what had been lost — that natural inclination toward family formation which had characterized the earlier and more virtuous periods of the republic.

The causes of such a disposition appear to be manifold. First, there is the tendency of commercial society to elevate individual comfort and convenience above the duties that bind us to posterity. Where men and women calculate the costs of child-rearing against the immediate pleasures of wealth and leisure, the calculation will often favor the present over the future. This is particularly true in periods of great prosperity, when the struggle for mere subsistence no longer compels families to view children as necessary hands for labor or support in old age.

Second, there is what I might term the democratical paradox of choice itself. In societies where marriage and procreation are matters of individual election rather than social necessity or family arrangement, the very abundance of alternatives may paralyze the choosing faculty. The citizen who possesses unlimited options for the employment of his time and resources may find himself unable to commit irrevocably to any single course — and the rearing of children is nothing if not an irrevocable commitment.

Third, and perhaps most troubling for republican government, there appears to be an inverse relationship between the expansion of individual rights and the acceptance of generational obligations. As citizens become more conscious of their personal liberty — their right to pursue happiness as they individually define it — they may become correspondingly less conscious of their duty to ensure that such liberty survives them. The very success of republican institutions in securing individual freedom may thus contain the seeds of their own dissolution.

The political consequences of such a trend are grave indeed. A republic requires not merely present citizens, but future ones — and not merely future ones, but future ones raised in the habits of self-government and civic virtue. If nearly half the adult population declines to participate in this great work of civic reproduction, the burden falls entirely upon the other half. This creates what I might call a faction of the fertile against a faction of the childless — a division that cuts across all other political distinctions and may prove more fundamental than any of them.

Moreover, a society that ceases to reproduce itself at replacement levels faces the prospect of being overwhelmed by societies that maintain more traditional patterns of family formation. This presents dangers both external and internal. Externally, such a republic may find itself unable to defend its territory or maintain its independence against more populous neighbors. Internally, it may discover that its political culture is gradually displaced by that of immigrant populations whose family structures and civic traditions differ markedly from those of the founding generation.

Yet I would caution against hasty remedies. The Roman example suggests that direct governmental interference in matters so intimate as marriage and procreation is both ineffective and dangerous to liberty itself. A republic that must coerce its citizens to reproduce has already ceased to be a republic in any meaningful sense. The family is the nursery of civic virtue precisely because it is voluntary — because it springs from natural affection rather than legal compulsion.

The remedy, if any exists, must be found in the restoration of those social conditions that make family formation appear both possible and desirable to the generality of citizens. This may require attention to the economic arrangements that govern society — ensuring that the rewards of industry are sufficient to support a household, that the costs of education and establishment do not consume the entire product of a man's labor, that the future appears sufficiently secure to justify the investment of twenty years in raising a child to independence.

It may also require what I can only call a revival of republican sentiment — a renewed appreciation for the truth that individual liberty depends upon collective continuance, and that the rights we enjoy were purchased by the sacrifices of our predecessors and must be preserved by our own sacrifices for our successors. This is not a truth that can be legislated into existence, but it may be cultivated through education, example, and the gradual influence of public opinion.

The question you pose thus reveals a tension inherent in republican government itself: between the individual liberty that is its end and the social reproduction that is its means. I cannot pretend to have resolved this tension, but I can say with confidence that a republic which fails to resolve it will not long endure. The generation that enjoys liberty without transmitting it has broken faith both with the past and with the future — and such a generation, however prosperous or enlightened it may consider itself, has failed in the first duty of republican citizenship.
